title: Risk / GRC / Assurance
audience: Risk, compliance, legal, privacy, and assurance roles.

questions.text: What is "algorithmic bias" in AI?
options: The process of selecting the best algorithm for a task when assessing AI governance and assurance.
Systematic and unfair discrimination by an AI system against certain groups or individuals.
A preference for certain algorithms over others as part of compliant AI lifecycle management.
The AI's inability to learn complex patterns when validating responsible AI practices.

questions.text: Why is "data privacy" a significant concern when handling data for AI models?
options: To speed up AI model training for legal, ethical, and operational risk control.
To keep data hidden from competitors as part of compliant AI lifecycle management within the same governance and assurance context.
To reduce the storage cost of data when validating responsible AI practices.
To prevent unauthorized access, use, or disclosure of personal and sensitive information.

questions.text: What does "transparency" generally refer to in AI systems from a governance perspective?
options: The extent to which humans can understand the internal workings and decision-making processes of an AI model.
Ensuring the AI's code is open-source when validating responsible AI practices.
The ability to inspect infrastructure diagrams without understanding model behaviour.
The AI's ability to communicate clearly using human language for legal, ethical, and operational risk control.

questions.text: In the context of AI risk management, what is a "false positive" when an AI system flags something?
options: The AI correctly identified a threat when validating responsible AI practices.
The AI failed to identify a real threat when assessing AI governance and assurance within the same governance and assurance context.
The AI incorrectly identified something as a threat (e.g., flagging a legitimate transaction as fraudulent).
The model produced a favourable result, regardless of whether the flag was accurate.

questions.text: Which of these is a common ethical principle for responsible AI development?
options: AI should bypass human oversight whenever possible.
AI should be designed to be explainable and fair.
AI should always prioritize profit maximization.
AI should be built privately until launch to avoid slowing the delivery timeline.

questions.text: What is a primary concern for legal professionals regarding AI-generated content?
options: The file size of the generated content for legal, ethical, and operational risk control.
The speed at which content is generated as part of compliant AI lifecycle management.
Ensuring generated content is polished and consistent with brand presentation.
Copyright ownership, intellectual property rights, and potential for defamation.

questions.text: What role does "auditing" play in ensuring responsible AI systems?
options: To train AI models on new audit data as part of compliant AI lifecycle management.
To evaluate AI systems for compliance with ethical guidelines, regulatory requirements, and performance standards.
To automatically fix all errors in AI models when assessing AI governance and assurance.
To count the number of AI models in an organization for legal, ethical, and operational risk control within the same governance and assurance context.

questions.text: What is "model interpretability" and why is it important for assurance professionals?
options: The ease with which an AI model's code can be read when validating responsible AI practices.
The ability to understand or explain why an AI model made a specific prediction or decision, crucial for validating fairness and compliance.
The model's ability to interpret complex data for legal, ethical, and operational risk control.
The process of translating AI model outputs into human language as part of compliant AI lifecycle management within the same governance and assurance context.

questions.text: Which regulatory framework specifically addresses AI systems' risks and aims to ensure their safety and fundamental rights?
options: HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act).
GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation).
The proposed EU AI Act.
PCI DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard).

questions.text: What is a "synthetic data" approach, and how can it mitigate privacy risks in AI development?
options: Encrypting all real data before it's used by AI for legal, ethical, and operational risk control within the same governance and assurance context.
Generating artificial data that mimics the statistical properties of real data without containing actual sensitive information.
Training AI models on real, sensitive data when validating responsible AI practices.
Deleting all personal data after model training when assessing AI governance and assurance.

questions.text: What is "adversarial machine learning" concerning security risks in AI?
options: A field dedicated to understanding and defending against malicious attacks that manipulate AI models (e.g., data poisoning, evasion attacks).
AI models helping humans in adversarial legal disputes when validating responsible AI practices within the same governance and assurance context.
Training AI models to compete against each other when assessing AI governance and assurance.
Models that produce adversarial language rather than being attacked by inputs.

questions.text: What does "model risk management" primarily aim to address?
options: The risk that a model becomes stale because it is not refreshed or monitored.
The potential for adverse consequences from decisions or outputs of AI models, encompassing financial, operational, and reputational risks.
The risk of losing AI model files for legal, ethical, and operational risk control within the same governance and assurance context.
The concern that a model gains broad access without defined control boundaries.

questions.text: Why is "data provenance" important in AI governance?
options: To determine the popularity of certain datasets when assessing AI governance and assurance.
To identify where old data from AI models comes from for legal, ethical, and operational risk control within the same governance and assurance context.
To track the origin, history, and transformations of data used in AI, ensuring its quality, integrity, and compliance.
To prove that AI models are from a specific region when validating responsible AI practices.

questions.text: In the context of AI compliance, what is "explainability as a service"?
options: An outsourced service for AI model training for legal, ethical, and operational risk control.
Tools or platforms that provide insights into an AI model's decisions, often integrated into a broader compliance framework.
A subscription service for explaining AI concepts to clients when validating responsible AI practices within the same governance and assurance context.
An AI model that explains its own code when assessing AI governance and assurance.

questions.text: Which statistical method can be used to assess and quantify "disparate impact" in AI models?
options: K-Means clustering used for grouping records rather than comparing outcomes across groups.
Simple Linear Regression when validating responsible AI practices.
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) when assessing AI governance and assurance within the same governance and assurance context.
Techniques like demographic parity, equal opportunity, or statistical parity metrics applied to model outcomes across groups.

questions.text: What is "differential privacy" and its relevance to AI?
options: A way to create different AI models for different levels of privacy when validating responsible AI practices.
A method to ensure AI model performance differs significantly between various datasets when assessing AI governance and assurance within the same governance and assurance context.
A technique to add noise to data or model outputs to provide strong privacy guarantees, protecting individual data points while allowing overall trends for AI analysis.
AI models that learn to keep data private on their own as part of compliant AI lifecycle management.

questions.text: When auditing an AI system, what does "counterfactual explanation" provide?
options: A narrative about how the AI model made its predictions when assessing AI governance and assurance.
A list of all historical predictions made by the AI model for legal, ethical, and operational risk control.
A statement of the minimal changes to features that would result in a different model prediction, aiding interpretability and fairness analysis.
An explanation of what would have happened if the AI model was trained on different data when validating responsible AI practices within the same governance and assurance context.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of a "sandbox environment" in AI development from a risk perspective?
options: To provide an isolated, secure environment for testing AI models with sensitive data without affecting production systems or exposing information.
To test models on artificial sample data without representing production risk.
To store AI models that are still under development when validating responsible AI practices within the same governance and assurance context.
To restrict AI models from accessing the internet when assessing AI governance and assurance.

questions.text: What does "model monitoring for data and concept drift" architecturally address from a quality assurance perspective?
options: The ongoing observation of model inputs and outputs to detect changes in data distribution or the underlying relationships (concepts), signaling a need for retraining or recalibration.
Tracking the version history of deployed AI models when validating responsible AI practices.
The physical movement of AI models between data centers when assessing AI governance and assurance within the same governance and assurance context.
Monitoring the cost of data storage for AI models for legal, ethical, and operational risk control.

questions.text: Which is a core component of an "AI governance framework"?
options: A list of all hardware used for AI when validating responsible AI practices.
A centralized database for all AI project plans when assessing AI governance and assurance within the same governance and assurance context.
A set of AI algorithms for managing data for legal, ethical, and operational risk control.
Policies, procedures, and oversight mechanisms to ensure AI systems are developed and used ethically, legally, and responsibly.
